title: Lysosomal Storage Diseases
authors: Robert J. Hopkin, Gregory A. Grabowski

items.title: Why Consider LSDs in Differential Diagnosis?
items.content: Lysosomal storage diseases (LSDs) should be considered when patients present with neurologic, renal, or muscular degeneration and/or unexplained hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, cardiomyopathy, or skeletal dysplasias. These conditions can manifest across a spectrum from severe to attenuated forms.
keyPoints: LSDs are rare diseases involving >50 different disorders.
Consider LSDs in differential diagnosis for neurologic, renal, muscular degeneration, unexplained hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, cardiomyopathy, or skeletal dysplasias.
Physical findings and enzyme assays or genetic testing can confirm the diagnosis.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.title: How to Diagnose LSDs?
items.content: Diagnosis of lysosomal storage diseases involves recognizing disease-specific physical findings, followed by confirmation through enzyme assays or genetic testing.
keyPoints: Physical findings are specific to each LSD.
Enzyme assays and genetic testing are definitive diagnostic tools for LSDs.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.title: Mechanisms of Lysosomal Dysfunction in LSDs
items.content: Lysosomal storage diseases (LSDs) arise from defects in the biosynthesis and function of lysosomal enzymes, leading to substrate accumulation. These abnormalities can occur at any step of enzyme synthesis or posttranslational modification, disrupting normal lysosomal activity.
keyPoints: Lysosomes originate from the fusion of trans-Golgi network vesicles with late endosomes.
Progressive acidification activates lysosomal hydrolases and facilitates receptor-ligand dissociation.
mTORC1 modulation regulates the balance between anabolic and catabolic pathways, affecting lysosome function.
Defects in posttranslational modifications can result in multiple enzyme/protein deficiencies.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Genetic Basis of LSDs
items.content: LSDs are primarily autosomal recessive disorders, with some X-linked and autosomal dominant exceptions. Mutations typically affect a single lysosomal hydrolase or protein subunit, but can also impact multiple enzymes by altering targeting or trafficking.
keyPoints: Most LSDs are inherited as autosomal recessive disorders.
Exceptions include Hunter (mucopolysaccharidosis type II), Danon, and Fabry diseases (X-linked) and Parry type neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (autosomal dominant).
Mutations can cause deficiencies of multiple lysosomal hydrolases by altering enzyme targeting or trafficking.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Threshold Hypothesis in LSDs
items.content: The threshold hypothesis suggests that disease manifests when enzyme activity falls below a critical level. Variability in substrate flux and genetic background can influence this threshold, leading to a spectrum of clinical manifestations.
keyPoints: Disease develops when lysosomal enzyme activity is below a certain threshold.
Small changes in enzyme activity near the threshold can modify disease presentation.
Enzymatic activity may be adequate for some tissues but not others due to varying substrate flux.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Screening for Tay-Sachs Disease in Ashkenazi Jews
items.content: Tay-Sachs disease is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by total hexosaminidase A deficiency due to $\alpha$-chain gene mutations. It presents in various forms: infantile, juvenile-onset, and adult-onset, each with distinct clinical features. The infantile form leads to death in infancy, while the juvenile and adult-onset forms have progressive neurological decline.
keyPoints: Carrier frequency is about 1 in 30 among Ashkenazi Jews.
Infantile form results in death during infancy.
Juvenile-onset presents with ataxia and dementia; death by age 10–15 years.
Adult-onset characterized by progressive motor weakness, spinocerebellar signs, dysarthria, and psychiatric disorders.
Screening for carriers is recommended in the Ashkenazi Jewish population.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Fabry Disease
items.content: Fabry disease, an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder, results from mutations in the GLA gene encoding $\alpha$-galactosidase A. It presents with angiokeratomas, hypohidrosis, corneal and lenticular opacities, acroparesthesia, and progressive organ involvement (kidney, heart, brain).
keyPoints: Prevalence in hemizygous males ranges from 1 in 40,000 to 1 in 3500.
Angiokeratomas are dark red to blue-black skin lesions, often overlooked.
Acroparesthesia can be triggered by temperature changes, exercise, fatigue, or fever.
Renal dysfunction occurs in the second to fourth decades; ~5% of 'idiopathic renal failure' cases have GLA mutations.
Cardiovascular complications include hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy.
1–3% of 'idiopathic hypertrophic myocardiopathy' patients have Fabry disease.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.title: Management Strategies for Fabry Disease
items.content: Early intervention in Fabry disease is crucial to prevent irreversible complications such as renal insufficiency, cardiac fibrosis, and stroke. Treatment options include enzyme replacement therapy (intravenous ET) and chaperone therapy (migalastat). Gabapentin and carbamazepine are used for acroparesthesia management.
keyPoints: Gabapentin and carbamazepine reduce chronic and episodic acroparesthesias.
Intravenous ET clears stored lipids from cells.
Chaperone therapy (migalastat) is effective for some patients with amenable mutations.
Renal insufficiency, cardiac fibrosis, and stroke are irreversible; early treatment is essential.
Chronic hemodialysis or kidney transplantation can be lifesaving in renal failure.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Gaucher Disease Type 1
items.content: Gaucher disease type 1, a nonneuronopathic form, presents with visceral symptoms that can appear in childhood to adulthood. It is characterized by hepatosplenomegaly and variable blood cytopenias, without direct correlation to organ size. Diagnosis is confirmed by decreased acid β-glucosidase activity (0–20% of normal) in nucleated cells.
keyPoints: Gaucher disease type 1 presents with visceral symptoms, often bimodally at <10–15 years and ~25 years.
Hepatosplenomegaly occurs in virtually all patients; blood cytopenias are variable.
Diagnosis is established by decreased acid β-glucosidase activity (0–20% of normal) in nucleated cells.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Genetic and Phenotypic Correlations in Gaucher Disease
items.content: The GBA1 gene mutations correlate with disease severity. The p.N370S mutation is associated with nonneuronopathic type 1 Gaucher disease, often resulting in later-onset/less severe manifestations. L444P homozygosity correlates with severe early-onset disease and potential CNS involvement.
keyPoints: p.N370S mutation (N370S/N370S or N370S/other) is linked to nonneuronopathic type 1 Gaucher disease.
L444P/L444P genotype often results in severe, early-onset disease with possible CNS involvement.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Management Strategies for Gaucher Disease
items.content: Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is the first-line treatment for significantly affected patients, reducing hepatosplenomegaly and improving hematologic values. Substrate reduction therapy (SRT), such as eliglustat tartrate, serves as an alternative or adjunctive treatment, especially when ERT is ineffective or contraindicated.
keyPoints: ERT is first-line for significantly affected patients, reducing hepatosplenomegaly and improving hematologic values.
SRT (eliglustat tartrate) is approved as a first-line therapy for adults; miglustat is a second-line option.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Gaucher Disease Type 3: Visceral vs. CNS Involvement
items.content: Gaucher disease type 3 presents with severe visceral involvement similar to type 1 but more pronounced, and variable CNS manifestations that can range from early childhood rapidly progressive disease to static or slowly progressive cognitive issues in adulthood.
keyPoints: Type 3 has highly variable CNS manifestations, including supranuclear lateral gaze palsy and potential for dementia or myoclonic seizures.
Visceral involvement is more severe than type 1 but responds to enzyme therapy (ET).
CNS involvement does not respond to ET.
More frequent among individuals of non-Western descent.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.title: Niemann-Pick Disease Types A and B: Clinical Course and Management
items.content: Type A Niemann-Pick disease presents early with rapid CNS deterioration, while type B has a later onset with progressive hepatosplenomegaly and potential pulmonary complications. Both types show decreased ASM activity, but only nonneurologic manifestations respond to ET.
keyPoints: Type A: Onset in the first 6 months of life, rapidly progressive CNS disease, spasticity, failure to thrive, massive hepatosplenomegaly.
Type B: Later onset with hepatosplenomegaly progressing to cirrhosis and pulmonary complications; some patients have slowly progressive CNS involvement (A/B variant).
Diagnosis by markedly decreased ASM activity (1-10% of normal) in nucleated cells.
ET improves nonneurologic manifestations but not neurologic ones.
Complications with HT or BMT/HSCT due to recurrence or lack of sphingomyelin clearance.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Mucopolysaccharidoses Type I (MPS I)
items.content: HSCT is the standard treatment for MPS I patients presenting at <2 years of age with or at risk for neurologic degeneration, while ET addresses hepatosplenomegaly and alleviates cardiac and respiratory disease. A combination of ET and HSCT may offer advantages.
keyPoints: HSCT stabilizes CNS disease, reverses hepatosplenomegaly, improves cardiac and respiratory disease but does not eliminate corneal or skeletal disease.
ET effectively addresses hepatosplenomegaly and alleviates cardiac and respiratory disease without affecting CNS.
Combination of ET and HSCT may reduce disease burden; experience with this approach is not well documented.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Mucopolysaccharidoses Type IV (Morquio Syndrome)
items.content: MPS IV presents as spondyloepiphyseal skeletal dysplasia with heart and respiratory complications. Elosulfase alfa is approved for MPS IVA, improving mobility and decreasing pain.
keyPoints: MPS IV prevalence: 1 in 200,000–300,000.
Type A caused by GALNS deficiency; Type B by acid β-galactosidase deficiency.
Elosulfase alfa approved for MPS IVA treatment improves ambulatory mobility and decreases pain.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.title: Management of Mucopolysaccharidoses Type VI (Maroteaux-Lamy Disease)
items.content: ET for MPS VI, caused by ARSB deficiency, is FDA approved. It addresses hepatosplenomegaly, bone disease, heart and respiratory issues without neurologic degeneration.
keyPoints: MPS VI characterized by hepatosplenomegaly, bone disease, heart disease, respiratory compromise, and short stature.
ET for MPS VI is FDA approved and treats visceral signs similar to MPS I but lacks CNS involvement.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.title: Recognizing Early-Onset Pompe Disease: Key Clinical Features and Urgency of Treatment
items.content: Early-onset Pompe disease, caused by GAA mutations leading to total enzyme deficiency, presents with hypotonia, myocardiopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and is rapidly progressive. Without treatment, it typically results in death within the first year.
keyPoints: Classic severe infantile form presents with hypotony, myocardiopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and enzyme total deficiency.
Rapid progression generally leads to death by age 1 without intervention.
ET approved for all ages prolongs life in the infantile form and improves cardiac function if started before 6 months.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.title: Late-Onset Pompe Disease: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: Late-onset Pompe disease, with an incidence as common as 1 in 40,000, presents with a slowly progressive myopathy resembling limb-girdle muscular dystrophy. Respiratory insufficiency may develop, requiring mechanical ventilation in advanced stages.
keyPoints: Incidence of late-onset variants is approximately 1 in 40,000.
Patients present with slowly progressive myopathy and possible respiratory insufficiency.
ET for late-onset forms can prevent deterioration if initiated early.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.title: Lysosomal Acid Lipase Deficiency: Diagnosis and Treatment Implications
items.content: Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency, caused by LIPA mutations, presents in severe infantile-onset form (Wolman syndrome) with failure to thrive and hepatosplenomegaly, leading to death without treatment. Childhood/adult-onset forms involve liver disease and atherosclerosis.
keyPoints: Infantile-onset LALD leads to death by age 1 (median age of death, 3.7 months) without treatment.
Childhood/adult-onset LALD can lead to early cirrhosis (~13 years) and atherosclerosis.
ET is effective in reversing disease manifestations across all ages.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.title: Early Presentation and Progression of Krabbe Disease
items.content: Krabbe disease is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by GALC deficiency, presenting in infancy with symptoms like hyperirritability, feeding problems, fever, seizures, and rapid progression. Early-onset form typically presents at 4 months and progresses to death around 18 months.
keyPoints: Caused by deficiency in $ \beta $-galactocerebrosidase (GALC).
Autosomal recessive disorder with panethnic distribution.
Early infantile form presents at an average age of 4 months.
Progresses rapidly, leading to death at an average age of 18 months.
Symptoms include hyperirritability, feeding problems, fever, seizures, and neurodegeneration.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · KRABBE DISEASE | NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2) | ALPHA MANNOSIDOSIS

items.subtopic: NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2)
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 2
items.content: CLN2, caused by TPP1 mutations, presents between ages 2-4 with seizures, ataxia, myoclonus, and vision loss. Diagnosis requires gene sequencing due to clinical overlap with other CLNs. Treatment involves cerliponase alfa administered via intracerebroventricular infusions every two weeks.
keyPoints: Due to mutations in TPP1 leading to tripeptidyl peptidase 1 deficiency.
Autosomal recessive disorder presenting between ages 2 and 4 years.
Symptoms include seizures, ataxia, myoclonus, vision loss, and motor skill losses.
Average age of death is around 12 years.
FDA/EMA-approved treatment: cerliponase alfa administered every 2 weeks via intracerebroventricular infusions.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · KRABBE DISEASE | NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2) | ALPHA MANNOSIDOSIS

items.question: Which enzyme assay or test is used for a definitive diagnosis of lysosomal storage diseases?
items.explanation: Enzyme assays or genetic testing are used to make a definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as an example of lysosomal storage diseases in the passage?
items.explanation: Huntington's disease is not mentioned as a lysosomal storage disease.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.question: What should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients with unexplained hepatomegaly or splenomegaly?
items.explanation: LSDs should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients with unexplained hepatomegaly or splenomegaly.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.question: Which process is involved in the maturation of lysosomal vesicles?
items.explanation: Progressive acidification accompanies the maturation of lysosomal vesicles.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is a common pathway for lysosomal storage diseases (LSDs)?
options: Deficiency in lysosomal hydrolases due to mutations
items.explanation: The majority of LSDs result from point mutations or genetic rearrangements affecting a single lysosomal hydrolase.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the inheritance pattern of most lysosomal storage diseases?
options: Most are inherited as autosomal dominant disorders.
Nearly all are inherited as autosomal recessive disorders.
They are typically mitochondrial in origin.
items.explanation: Nearly all LSDs are inherited as autosomal recessive disorders, with some exceptions like Hunter and Fabry diseases that are X-linked.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is the role of mTORC1 in lysosomal function?
options: It regulates transcription factors TFEB/TFE3 for anabolic and catabolic balance.
It prevents substrate accumulation within lysosomes.
items.explanation: mTORC1 modulates the transcription factors TFEB/TFE3, affecting the balance between anabolic and catabolic pathways in lysosome function.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is responsible for Tay-Sachs disease?
items.explanation: Tay-Sachs disease results from total Hexosaminidase A (Hex A) deficiency.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.question: What is the primary genetic inheritance pattern of Fabry disease?
items.explanation: Fabry disease is an X-linked disorder.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is characteristic of Tay-Sachs disease?
items.explanation: A macular cherry red spot is a characteristic symptom of Tay-Sachs disease.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.question: In Fabry disease, which organ system's dysfunction typically occurs in the second to fourth decades?
items.explanation: Progressive renal dysfunction is a manifestation of Fabry disease occurring in the second to fourth decades.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency causes Gaucher disease?
items.explanation: Gaucher disease results from defective activity of acid β-glucosidase.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which mutation is most commonly associated with nonneuronopathic Gaucher disease in the Ashkenazi Jewish population?
items.explanation: The p.N370S mutation is most commonly associated with nonneuronopathic or type 1 Gaucher disease in the Ashkenazi Jewish population.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of Gaucher disease type 1?
items.explanation: Hepatosplenomegaly occurs in virtually all clinically identified patients with Gaucher disease type 1.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment is considered first-line for significantly affected Gaucher disease patients?
items.explanation: Regular intravenous enzyme therapy is the first-line treatment for significantly affected Gaucher disease patients.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which type of Gaucher disease typically presents with rapidly progressive, massive visceral disease and slowly progresses to static CNS involvement?
items.explanation: Gaucher disease type 3 can present in early childhood with rapidly progressive, massive visceral disease and slowly progress to static CNS involvement.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.question: Which form of Niemann-Pick disease is characterized by early onset in the first 6 months of life with rapidly progressive CNS deterioration?
items.explanation: Niemann-Pick disease type A typically has its onset in the first 6 months of life with rapidly progressive CNS deterioration.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.question: Which treatment for Niemann-Pick C disease is currently in clinical trials?
items.explanation: Substrate depletion with cyclodextrin is in clinical trials for Niemann-Pick C disease.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.question: Which of the following treatments has been shown to stabilize CNS disease in patients with MPS I?
items.explanation: HSCT results in stabilization of CNS disease in MPS I.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.question: Which mucopolysaccharidosis is characterized by the absence of corneal clouding?
items.explanation: Hunter disease (MPS II) does not have corneal clouding.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.question: What is the primary enzyme deficiency in MPS IV type A?
items.explanation: MPS IV type A is caused by a deficiency in N-acetyl-galactosamine-6-sulfatase (GALNS).
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.question: Which treatment has been approved for the visceral manifestations of MPS II?
items.explanation: The FDA and EMA have approved ET for the visceral manifestations of MPS II.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of the classic severe infantile form of Pompe disease?
options: Late-onset myopathy resembling limb-girdle muscular dystrophy
Respiratory insufficiency as an initial presenting sign
Hypotonia, myocardiopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly with total enzyme deficiency
Absence of myocardiopathy in late-onset variants
items.explanation: The classic severe infantile form presents with hypotonia, myocardiopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly, along with a total deficiency of the enzyme.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.question: What is the primary benefit of early intervention with acid $ \alpha $-glucosidase enzyme therapy (ET) in late-onset Pompe disease?
items.explanation: Early intervention with acid $ \alpha $-glucosidase ET may limit or prevent deterioration, although very advanced disease will have significant irreversible components.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.question: Which of the following is true about enzyme therapy (ET) for infantile-onset lysosomal acid lipase deficiency?
options: It has no effect on disease progression.
It can reverse disease manifestations and is approved by regulatory agencies.
items.explanation: ET for lysosomal acid lipase deficiency reverses disease manifestations and is approved for patients at all ages by the EMA, FDA, and several other country agencies.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.question: What is a common initial presentation in childhood/adult-onset lysosomal acid lipase deficiency?
options: Elevated liver enzymes and microvesicular fatty liver disease
Severe failure to thrive with vomiting
items.explanation: Childhood/adult-onset lysosomal acid lipase deficiency often involves elevated liver enzymes, microvesicular fatty liver disease, and cryptogenic cirrhosis.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · KRABBE DISEASE | NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2) | ALPHA MANNOSIDOSIS

items.statement: All lysosomal storage diseases exhibit a single, uniform phenotype.
items.explanation: Each disease exhibits a spectrum of manifestations from severe to attenuated variants.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.statement: There are more than 50 different lysosomal storage diseases.
items.explanation: The passage states there are >50 different lysosomal storage diseases.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · Lysosomal Storage Diseases

items.statement: The threshold hypothesis suggests that small changes in enzyme activity near a critical level can lead to or modify disease.
items.explanation: Small changes in enzyme activity near the threshold can indeed lead to or modify disease.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: All lysosomal storage diseases are inherited as autosomal recessive disorders.
items.explanation: Some LSDs, like Hunter and Fabry diseases, are X-linked, and others such as Parry type neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis can be autosomal dominant.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Sandhoff disease and Tay-Sachs disease are phenotypically identical.
items.explanation: Sandhoff disease is similar to Tay-Sachs but includes additional symptoms like hepatosplenomegaly.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.statement: Males with Fabry disease always exhibit more severe manifestations than females.
items.explanation: Females can have variable manifestations due to Lyonization effects, and heart disease is the most common life-threatening manifestation in females.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.statement: Severe liver dysfunction is common in Gaucher disease type 1.
items.explanation: Severe liver dysfunction is unusual, but progressive cirrhosis can occur.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: The N370S/N370S genotype always presents with clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: As many as 40% of individuals with the N370S/N370S genotype do not present clinically.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Visceral involvement in Gaucher disease type 3 responds to enzyme therapy.
items.explanation: Visceral—but not CNS—involvement in Gaucher disease type 3 responds to ET.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.statement: Niemann-Pick disease type B is characterized by early onset and rapidly progressive CNS disease.
items.explanation: Type B Niemann-Pick disease has a later, more variable onset without the rapid progression of CNS disease seen in type A.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.statement: HSCT is effective in treating CNS disease associated with MPS II.
items.explanation: HSCT has not been successful in treating CNS disease associated with MPS II.

items.statement: ET for Maroteaux-Lamy disease (MPS VI) is FDA approved.
items.explanation: Enzyme therapy for MPS VI has received FDA approval.

items.assertion: Lysosomal enzyme deficiencies commonly result from point mutations or genetic rearrangements.
items.reason: These mutations affect a locus encoding a single lysosomal hydrolase or protein subunit.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: The substrate accumulations in lysosomal storage diseases lead to significant pathophysiologic consequences.
items.reason: These accumulations cause lysosomal distortions and dysfunctions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Early institution of therapy for Fabry disease can prevent or slow progression of complications.
items.reason: Renal insufficiency, cardiac fibrosis, and stroke are irreversible in Fabry disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early therapy is beneficial.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.assertion: The juvenile-onset form of Tay-Sachs disease presents with ataxia and dementia.
items.reason: Juvenile-onset Tay-Sachs results from mutations in the beta-chain gene.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because juvenile-onset Tay-Sachs results from alpha-chain gene mutations.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · TAY-SACHS DISEASE | FABRY DISEASE

items.assertion: Bone marrow involvement in Gaucher disease can lead to vertebral collapse.
items.reason: Gaucher cells infiltrate bone marrow, causing infarction and necrosis which spread from proximal to distal in the limbs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: The most common mutation in Gaucher disease is p.L444P.
items.reason: p.L444P is associated with very low enzyme activity and severe early-onset disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the most common mutation is p.N370S, not p.L444P.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Niemann-Pick disease type A typically has its onset in the first 6 months of life.
items.reason: Type A is characterized by rapidly progressive CNS deterioration and failure to thrive.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · GAUCHER DISEASE (part 2) | NIEMANN-PICK DISEASES

items.assertion: Regular follow-up is required throughout the lives of patients with MPS I even after successful HSCT.
items.reason: Late cardiac and respiratory complications are being reported in patients with MPS I.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why regular follow-up is necessary.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.assertion: ET for MPS IVB has been approved.
items.reason: There is no specific treatment for MPS IVB.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; there is no current specific treatment for MPS IVB.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · MUCOPOLYSACCHARIDOSES

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.assertion: Early intervention with enzyme therapy in Pompe disease can improve respiratory function.
items.reason: Respiratory function is improved in most treated infants if instituted before age 6 months.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY
items.assertion: Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency primarily affects the central nervous system.
items.reason: Neither clinical variant of lysosomal acid lipase deficiency manifests primary CNS disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · POMPE DISEASE | LYSOSOMAL ACID LIPASE DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: KRABBE DISEASE | NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2) | ALPHA MANNOSIDOSIS
items.assertion: Gene therapy has shown dramatic improvement in neurologic function and survival in Krabbe disease.
items.reason: Studies in mouse and dog models of Krabbe disease have demonstrated these improvements.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Lysosomal Storage Diseases · KRABBE DISEASE | NEURONAL CEROID LIPOFUSCINOSIS TYPE 2 (NCL2 OR CLN2) | ALPHA MANNOSIDOSIS
